The food supplies of Israel, brought from Egypt, were used up by the middle of the second month, and they complained. It is no new thing for the greatest kindness to be portrayed as the greatest injuries. They so undervalued their deliverance that they wished they had died in Egypt; and by the hand of the Lord, that is, by the plagues which cut off the Egyptians. We cannot suppose they had plenty in Egypt, nor could they fear dying from lack in the wilderness, while they had flocks and herds: those who complain are never reasonable. When we begin to fret, we should consider that God hears all our complaints. God promises a quick and constant supply. He tested whether they would trust him and be satisfied with bread for the day on that day. In this way he tested if they would serve him, and it became clear how ungrateful they were. When God plagued the Egyptians, it was to make them know he was their Lord; when he provided for the Israelites, it was to make them know he was their God.

In the evening, quails came up, and the people easily caught as many as they needed. Manna fell in dew. They called it “Manna, Manhu,” which means, “What is this?” “It is a portion; it is that which our God has allotted us, and we will take it and be thankful.” It was pleasant food; it was wholesome food. The manna rained from heaven; it appeared when the dew was gone as small, round things, like hoar frost, like coriander seed in color, like pearls. The manna fell only six days a week, and in double quantity on the sixth day; it bred worms and became offensive if kept more than one day, except on the Sabbath. The people had never seen it before. It could be ground in a mill or beaten in a mortar and then made into cakes and baked. It continued for the forty years that the Israelites were in the wilderness, wherever they went, and ceased when they arrived in Canaan. All this shows how different it was from anything found before or found now. They were to gather the manna every morning. We are taught by this, 1. To be prudent and diligent in providing food for ourselves and our households; working quietly and eating our own bread, not the bread of idleness or deceit. God’s bounty leaves room for human duty; it did so even when manna rained; they must not eat until they have gathered. 2. To be content with enough. Those who have most have only food and clothing for themselves; those who have least generally have these; so that he who gathers much has nothing over, and he who gathers little has no lack. There is not such a disproportion between one and another in the enjoyment of the things of this life as in the mere possession of them. 3. To depend upon Providence: let them sleep quietly even if they have no bread in their tents or throughout their camp, trusting that God would bring them their daily bread with the following day. It was safer in God’s storehouse than their own and would come from there sweeter and fresher. See here the folly of hoarding. The manna laid up by some, who thought themselves wiser and better managers than their neighbors, and who wanted to provide for a possible shortage next day, bred worms and became useless. That which is covetously and distrustfully spared will be wasted. Such riches are corrupted. The same wisdom, power, and goodness that brought food daily from above for the Israelites in the wilderness also brings food yearly out of the earth through the constant course of nature and gives us all things richly to enjoy.

Here is mention of a seventh-day Sabbath. It was known not only before the giving of the law on Mount Sinai but before Israel was brought out of Egypt, from the beginning. Setting apart one day in seven for holy work and, in order to that, for holy rest has been done ever since God created man on earth, and is the most ancient of the Divine laws. By appointing them to rest on the seventh day, he made sure they would not lose anything by it; and no one ever will be a loser by serving God. On that day they were to bring in enough for two days and make it ready. This directs us to arrange family affairs so that they hinder us as little as possible in the work of the Sabbath. Works of necessity are to be done on that day, but it is desirable to do as little as possible so we can apply ourselves more closely to preparing for the life to come. When they kept manna against a command, it stank; when they kept it by a command, it was sweet and good; everything is sanctified by the word of God and prayer. On the seventh day God did not send the manna, so they should not expect it or go out to gather it. This showed that it was produced by miracle.

God having provided manna to be his people’s food in the wilderness, the memory of it must be preserved. Eaten bread must not be forgotten. God's miracles and mercies are to be remembered. The word of God is the manna by which our souls are nourished. The comforts of the Spirit are hidden manna. These come from heaven as the manna did, and are the support and comfort of spiritual life in the soul while we are in the wilderness of this world. Christ in the word must be applied to the soul, and the means of grace must be used. Each one of us must gather for ourselves and gather in the morning of our days, the morning of our opportunities; if we let these slip, it may be too late to gather them. The manna must not be hoarded but eaten; those who have received Christ must by faith live upon him and not receive his grace in vain. There was enough manna for all, enough for each, and no one had too much; so in Christ there is enough, but not more than we need. But those who ate manna hungered again, died at last, and were not pleased with God; whereas those who feed on Christ by faith will never hunger and will die no more, and God will be forever well pleased with them. Let us seek earnestly for the grace of the Holy Spirit to turn all our knowledge of the doctrine of Christ crucified into spiritual nourishment for our souls by faith and love.
